nativeRange.habitat: Shallow to mid-depth reef slopes, reef walls, and protected reef zones; typically found plating outward on horizontal or near-horizontal surfaces at moderate depths

decorPreferences: stable rockwork base for plating or encrusting growth
horizontal or gently angled surface in middle to lower rockwork
ample open space around the colony (10–15 cm clearance from neighbors)

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Target feeding once or twice weekly with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, baby brine shrimp, rotifers, copepods, or Reef Roids tends to support growth and coloration. A turkey baster or pipette is generally advisable for targeted delivery. Feeding tentacles are typically extended at night, making evening feeding generally the most effective approach.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals placed well out of sweeper tentacle reach
avoidWith: corals within sweeper tentacle range (8–10 cm)
other Mycedium colonies without adequate spacing
aggressive LPS such as Galaxea or Hammer placed nearby
high-flow powerheads directed at colony surface

summary: Mycedium elephantotus (Elephant Skin Chalice) is a plating LPS coral in family Merulinidae, sold as part of the 'Chalice Coral' trade umbrella. Generally intermediate care; prefers moderate light, low-to-moderate flow, and spacing from neighbors.
careNotes:
Chalice trade-umbrella note: 'Chalice Coral' is a broad commercial name applied to several genera. Within that umbrella, the true chalice clade — Echinophyllia (see [echinophyllia-coral]) and Oxypora (see [oxypora-coral]) — belongs to family Lobophylliidae. Mycedium, despite sharing the chalice trade name and very similar plating growth form, belongs to family Merulinidae (confirmed per WoRMS 2012 molecular revision by Budd et al.). This makes Mycedium more closely related to Echinopora (see [echinopora-coral], also Merulinidae) than to Echinophyllia or Oxypora — the shared chalice trade name and growth form reflect convergent morphology rather than close phylogenetic relationship. Older sources (pre-2012) may list Mycedium in family Pectiniidae.

Distinguishing features: Mycedium elephantotus has a characteristic appearance with corallites that tilt outward and upward from the colony surface (giving the 'elephant skin' or 'elephant nose' texture), with eyes that protrude visibly. This is a useful field-identification feature distinguishing it from Echinophyllia (where corallites sit flush or are slightly sunken) and Oxypora (very thin-plated with small, evenly spaced eyes).

Lighting: Sources generally converge on 75–150 PAR as the working range for Mycedium. MetaReef notes Mycedium tolerates slightly higher intensities than other chalice genera — up to 150–200 PAR when acclimated gradually. New specimens should be introduced at lower PAR (50–75) and gradually acclimated over 2–3 weeks to prevent bleaching. Avoid placement too close to the light source; indirect or diffused illumination is generally preferred.

Flow: Low to moderate indirect flow is generally advised. Strong direct flow can damage tissue and prevent sweeper tentacles from extending for feeding. MetaReef recommends 20–40× tank volume per hour in gentle, non-laminar circulation. ReefChasers characterizes the general chalice flow requirement as 'moderate.'

Aggression: Mycedium extends sweeper tentacles at night — commonly cited at several inches (sources give 'several inches' to '3–6 inches'). The 8–10 cm aggression range used here is an averaged conservative estimate consistent with the task specification (5–10 cm). Placement must account for full nighttime extension; 10–15 cm clearance from other corals is advisable.

Colony size: In the wild, M. elephantotus can form very large colonies — TFH Magazine reports colonies of 6–10 feet (180–300 cm) across on undisturbed reefs. Captive specimens are typically constrained to 15–60 cm before tank limits are reached. ReefLex lists maximum captive size of approximately 60 cm.

Depth range: ReefLex records a natural depth range of 3–70 m, indicating the species tolerates a wide range of light intensities in the wild and can adapt to varied tank positions.

Water quality: Stable parameters are important. Standard LPS reef chemistry is generally appropriate. Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium supplementation consistent with other LPS corals is recommended. Nitrate around 2–10 ppm and phosphate 0.03–0.1 ppm are broadly workable; extremely low-nutrient systems may cause color loss.

Parameter notes — cross-source summary: Temperature 23–27°C averaged from TFH (74–83°F), ReefLex (18–28°C, likely including shipping tolerance), and ReefChasers/ReefBuilders (77–84°F and 78°F); kH 8–9 dKH averaged from TFH (2.5–3.0 meq/L ≈ 7–8.4 dKH) and ReefChasers (8–9 dKH); salinity 1.023–1.026 from ExtremeCorals (1.023–1.025) and TFH/ReefBuilders (1.025–1.026); calcium 380–450 averaged from TFH (380–430) and ReefBuilders/ReefChasers (450 and 430–460).

Fragging: Fragging is the standard propagation method in captivity. Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a band saw; frags of 3+ cm encrust and recover well on frag plugs or rubble. Dipping new frags before introduction is advisable to prevent hitchhiker pests.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. Sexual broadcast spawning occurs on wild reefs but is not practiced in home aquaria. ReefLex notes that captive breeding is possible in principle, though offspring from sexual reproduction are rarely available in the trade.

primary.name: Extreme Corals – How to Care for Mycedium in a Reef Tank

additional.name: TFH Magazine – Mycedium elephantotus
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (74–83°F), salinity (1.025), pH (8.1–8.2), alkalinity (2.5–3.0 meq/L ≈ 7–8.4 dKH), calcium (380–430 ppm), native range (Indo-West Pacific, Red Sea, Australia to Tahiti, southern Japan), and wild colony size (6–10 feet). Provided taxonomy note: historical family Pectiniidae. Confirmed nocturnal feeding tentacle extension.

additional.name: MetaReef – Chalice Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked PAR range (50–150, Mycedium tolerating up to 200), flow (20–40× turnover, low to moderate indirect), care level (intermediate), and aggression (minimum 3–6 inches spacing). Provided genus-level PAR differentiation notes.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Chalice Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (8.0–8.4), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), calcium (430–460 ppm), PAR (75–100 general, Mycedium noted as tolerating higher than Echinophyllia or Oxypora), flow (moderate), care level (intermediate). Confirmed weekly spot feeding recommendation.

additional.name: ReefLex – Mycedium elephantotus
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (18–28°C — wide range likely includes shipping tolerance; conservative mid-range used), tank size (150L minimum), depth range (3–70 m), maximum captive size (60 cm), and native range. Taxonomy confirmation (family context).

additional.name: WoRMS – Mycedium (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: confirmed family Merulinidae (not Lobophylliidae as sometimes listed in hobby sources), order Scleractinia, suborder Vacatina. Historical placement in Pectiniidae confirmed as pre-2012 revision. Not used for husbandry parameters.
